quick: One of the largest true bulbs in the plant kingdom — a single underground bulb can weigh up to 40 pounds.

aliases: Callistemon viminalis (former botanical name, still widely used)
quick: The showy red "bottlebrush" is not made of petals — it's a burst of pollen-bearing stamens.
One of the rarest palms in the United States — the Elliott Key population — the last in Florida — numbers fewer than 50 individuals.

aliases: Chorisia speciosa (former botanical name, still widely used in horticulture)
quick: The conical spines on the trunk are thought to be defenses left over from now-extinct South American megafauna — giant ground sloths and gomphotheres.

Its leaves contain corosolic acid, a compound shown in clinical studies to lower blood sugar — making leaf tea a traditional diabetes treatment across the Philippines and India, where it's known as the Banabá plant.

aliases: Jatropha pandurifolia (former botanical name, still widely used)
quick: Every part of this plant is poisonous, yet it's one of the most widely planted ornamentals in South Florida — the red flowers bloom almost continuously and butterflies can't resist them.

aliases: Jacaranda acutifolia (former name, still seen in older sources)
quick: When a Jacaranda blooms, it doesn't hold anything back — the entire canopy goes purple-blue before a single leaf appears, creating one of the most theatrical flowering displays in the botanical world.
Florida's own native sunflower, perfectly adapted to the harsh conditions of coastal dunes where most plants refuse to grow.
Named Firewheel for obvious reasons, this wildflower manages to look like it's perpetually on fire.

The Geiger Tree is so associated with Key West that John James Audubon painted his famous White-crowned Pigeon sitting in one during his 1832 Florida expedition — the painting put both the bird and the tree on the ornithological map.
The African Baobab can live for over a thousand years and store tens of thousands of gallons of water in its massive trunk — making it a literal life-support system for wildlife during dry seasons.
Carl Linnaeus thought it was so beautiful he gave it the species name pulcherrima — Latin for 'most beautiful.'
Touch its leaves and they fold shut in seconds — a defensive response that confuses insects and startles almost everyone who walks past it.
The rough, sandpaper-textured leaves give it one of its folk names — Sandpaper Vine.
The scarlet tubular flowers bloom in step with the spring migration of Ruby-throated Hummingbirds moving north along the Gulf Coast.
The leaves do what flowers usually do — they're the show. And they intensify in the sun.
An iconic native palm of the Everglades and South Florida wetlands — its presence is a living marker of healthy, wet native habitat.
The peeling red-green bark is so distinctive it earned this tree one of Florida's best nicknames: the Tourist Tree — because the bark is always red and peeling.
Fewer than 10 to 15 mature individuals survive in the wild on a single tiny island — yet this is one of the most commonly planted ornamental palms in South Florida.
The Canary Islands were named after dogs, not birds — from the Latin Canariae Insulae, 'Islands of the Dogs.' The canary bird was named after the islands.
Not actually a palm — more closely related to the White Bird of Paradise growing twenty feet away in this same collection, and to Heliconia.
This plant contains bilirubin — a pigment normally found only in animals, produced when red blood cells break down. It is one of the very few plants on earth verified to contain it.
The oldest known Bald Cypress — and the oldest living tree in eastern North America — is 2,624 years old, growing along the Black River in North Carolina.
Florida's only native cycad — and the only native cycad in all of North America. Cycads as a group have barely changed in 300 million years.
Only female trees produce the berries — and only if a male tree is nearby for pollination. One male can serve multiple females within about 50 feet.

Every October, Muhly Grass produces a cloud of pink-purple seed plumes so fine they seem to float — the effect is stunning enough that highway departments plant it in miles of roadside medians across Florida.
A single large Live Oak can support over 500 species of caterpillars — more than almost any other tree genus in North America. Those caterpillars are what most songbirds feed their nestlings.

The seeds germinate on the tree and fall into the water as fully developed torpedo-shaped seedlings up to 12 inches long — they can float for over a year before taking root, crossing entire ocean basins.
The name is literal — when an alligator swims through a stand of Alligator Flag in the Everglades, the tall waving leaves trace its path through the marsh. Wildlife trackers read the flag.
Fakahatchee Grass shares a botanical subtribe with corn's wild ancestor, and the two can hybridize — its tiny seed spike looks like a miniature ear of early corn.

Dutch elm disease, carried by a bark beetle introduced in the 1920s, eliminated the vast arching elm canopies that once lined American streets from coast to coast — one of the most dramatic ecological losses in North American urban history.
Spanish shipbuilders prized this wood for hulls that shrugged off cannon fire, shipworms, and rot — long before Chippendale turned it into the prized furniture of Georgian England.
The Wild Lime is the essential host plant for the Giant Swallowtail butterfly — Florida's largest butterfly, with a wingspan reaching six inches.
Florida's only native Canna, this wetland wildflower produces large yellow blooms almost cartoonishly tropical for a plant that evolved right here.
Explodes into bloom with thick, powdery clouds of white pollen that look exactly like a winter snowstorm swirling through the tropical garden.
When a Royal Poinciana blooms, it earns its other name — Flamboyant Tree. The canopy can be entirely covered in vermillion flowers, visible from blocks away.
One of Florida's problem invaders: birds eat the red berries and drop the seeds in wild hammocks, where a seedling can take root high in a native tree and slowly strangle it.

aliases: (also sold as Ruellia brittoniana, R. tweediana, R. caerulea)
quick: Mexican Petunia is one of Florida's most commonly planted purple-flowered ornamentals — and one of its most problematic invasives.

Stand by the office and you're being welcomed by a milkweed — this is the big silver-leaved shrub that greets visitors coming up from the parking lot, and another large one rings the butterfly garden.

In our cactus garden you can see this plant's entire life story at once: young rosettes still building energy, a living giant with its towering bloom stalk, and a fully browned skeleton that has finished the job and died.

aliases: (hybrids often sold as Canna x generalis)
quick: Despite the name, Canna Lilies are not lilies at all — they're the showy tropical relatives of bananas and gingers.
The flower spike coils like a scorpion's tail and slowly uncurls as each tiny white bloom opens in turn, from base to tip.
Despite the name, this isn't a true iris — it's an African cousin in the iris family, built tough enough to shrug off drought, flooding, shade, and salt spray with equal indifference.

Despite the name, the Screw Pine is no pine at all — it's a monocot, more closely related to grasses, palms, and orchids than to any conifer. The 'screw' comes from the spiral of leaves winding up its trunk.
That big sky-blue flowering bush near the front entrance is Plumbago — and at Palma Sola it's an unusually large one, anchoring Welcome Island as you come in.
Snake Plants are sprinkled all around the park in many shapes and sizes — spotting them in their variety makes a fun scavenger hunt as you walk.
Grows on trees, not soil — this entire colony is clinging to a palm trunk the way its ancestors clung to rainforest canopy.
Recently reclassified from Philodendron into its own genus, the Xanadu is still adjusting to its new scientific identity.
Not a true lily — the name is a coincidence of appearance. It belongs to the aroid family, alongside philodendrons, anthuriums, and the park's own Xanadu.
The velvety red spikes — up to 18 inches long — have no petals at all; their color comes from masses of tiny red female flowers.
Flowers on bare branches — the tree drops all its leaves before blooming in spring, so the flowers have nothing to compete with.

aliases: Senecio confusus (former botanical name, still widely used)
quick: Belongs to the daisy family — Asteraceae — which makes those orange flowers composites: each apparent bloom is actually dozens of tiny individual flowers packed together.
The genus name Pentas is Greek for five — each flower has exactly five petals, five sepals, and five stamens, without exception.
Leaves can reach three feet across — round, stiff, and heavy. The fallen ones get picked up by staff regularly. Visitors sometimes ask if they can take one home.

A Florida native — and the original type specimen that first gave this plant its scientific identity was collected from Tampa Bay in the 1840s by a US Army surgeon named Hulse. The species is named for him.
The showy orange bracts are not flowers — they are modified leaves. The actual flowers are the small structures tucked inside them. The bracts last for weeks after the true flowers have finished.

aliases: Southern Bayberry; Formerly classified as Myrica cerifera
quick: The berries are coated in aromatic wax — the same wax colonial Americans boiled off to make bayberry candles. The candles burn cleaner than tallow and smell better. The tradition survives; bayberry candles are still sold today.
The largest fern in Florida, and found only in Florida among the 50 states — its cold sensitivity limits it to the peninsula and the Keys.

The name does what plant names rarely do — it tells you exactly what you will see. Callicarpa is Greek for beautiful fruit. When the berries are on, the name is self-evidently correct. Kids remember it.

The fiber stuffed inside the seed pods is hollow, making it buoyant and water-resistant. It was the primary filling for life jackets used by the US Navy through both World Wars before synthetics replaced it.
The flower lasts only a single morning — each bloom opens at dawn and is gone by noon. The plant compensates by producing them in succession over a long season.
The large yellow flower clusters and bold compound leaves make this one of the more visually assertive shrubs in the collection — hard to walk past without noticing.

The flowers are orange-red, but the structure that makes this plant striking is the pagoda-like tiered arrangement of the flower clusters — each cluster stacked above the last in diminishing rings, like a Buddhist temple tower.

The standard ixora is red — Jungle Flame is a common nickname. The Maui Yellow cultivar trades that for a soft golden-yellow that reads differently in the landscape: warmer, less assertive, more quietly tropical.
Not bamboo. Not even distantly related to bamboo. The resemblance is in the cane-like stems and compound leaves — the botany is completely different.

The genus Sagittaria takes its name from the Latin for arrow — most of its members have arrowhead-shaped leaves. This one is the exception: its leaves are narrow and lance-shaped, which is just what 'lancifolia' means.
One of the most medically significant plants in the world — the source of vincristine and vinblastine, chemotherapy drugs used against leukemia and Hodgkin's lymphoma.

aliases: Also called golden fig, higueron, or simply strangler fig. Florida's Native peoples are documented to have used the latex sap for chewing gum and the stems and roots for tools, cordage, and medicine.
quick: Starts life as a seed deposited by a bird in the fork or bark of another tree. Sends roots downward. Over years those roots reach the soil, thicken, and gradually enclose the host trunk. The host eventually dies, leaving the fig standing hollow around where it used to be.

One of Florida's three classic scrub oaks, and the one with the smallest leaves. In scrub habitat it regenerates from underground stems after fire — the above-ground plant burns, the root system survives, and new shoots emerge within weeks.
The real attraction is not the flower but what follows it — a large, waxy, inflated calyx in deep red-purple that persists and swells as the fruit develops inside. The calyx is the show.
Carries immense cultural significance across Polynesia as a sacred symbol of protection, prosperity, and agriculture.

Florida has a genuine claim on this plant — it is native here, though its nativity within Florida is debated. Historically abundant in the Florida Keys and coastal hammocks; now rare in the wild here, though common in cultivation throughout the tropics.

Despite the name, this is not a climbing vine — it is a shrub. Most of its hundred-odd Thunbergia relatives are vigorous climbers. King's Mantle stayed low and bushy, which is why it is manageable in a garden setting where the climbing species are not.

Firebush is one of the most important native plants in South Florida for wildlife — hummingbirds, butterflies, and more than two dozen bird species use it for nectar, fruit, or cover through the entire year.
That enormous burgundy bloom on the nursery fence is the Brazilian Dutchman's Pipe — a velvety, ivory-veined flower up to a foot or two long, shaped like an old smoking pipe.

The showy color is not the flower — it is the bract, a modified leaf surrounding the tiny actual flower inside. The real flowers are inconspicuous cream-white tubes at the bract center. The papery texture of the bracts is why the plant is called paper flower around the world.

The climbing relative of the park's King's Mantle — same genus, completely different growth habit. Where King's Mantle stays compact and bushy, Bengal Trumpet climbs vigorously and will cover a fence, pergola, or tree if given the opportunity.

A Florida native milkweed and a documented Monarch butterfly host plant. The caterpillars eat the leaves exclusively and sequester the toxic cardiac glycosides into their own bodies — making the adult butterflies poisonous to birds, which learn to avoid them.
Grows faster than almost any tree in Florida — a papaya planted from seed can produce fruit in under a year.

The flower opens once, at night, and is gone by morning. It is among the largest cactus flowers in the world — up to a foot across — white, fragrant, and pollinated by bats and moths while most visitors are asleep.

Not a moss. Not a parasite. Ball moss is a bromeliad — a relative of the pineapple and the colorful bromeliads in the park's collection — that anchors to bark and wire using specialized root-like structures called holdfasts.

The leaflets look like the ragged end of a torn piece of paper — or, if you squint, like the tail of a fish. No other palm has quite this leaf texture, which makes identification easy even from a distance.
Don't let the name fool you — the Madagascar Palm is no palm at all. It's a succulent with a fat, water-storing trunk, more closely related to milkweed and oleander than to any palm.

Native to sub-Saharan Africa, where it grows in clumps along rivers and streams. The multiple trunks lean outward from the base — the reclining habit that gives it its name — creating a sculptural form different from the typical upright palm.

One of the most shade-tolerant palms in cultivation — grows under closed forest canopy in its native range in southern China, which makes it genuinely useful for shaded positions where most palms fail.

The leaves contain more vitamin C than oranges, more calcium than milk, more iron than spinach, and more potassium than bananas — gram for gram. This is not marketing; it is documented nutritional analysis used in international food security programs.

The fragrance of plumeria flowers is one of the most recognizable scents in the tropical world — the base of Hawaiian lei culture, a fixture in tropical perfumery, and the scent most visitors associate with warm-climate vacations.

The name Porterweed comes from the Caribbean tradition of boiling and fermenting the leaves into a dark, foaming medicinal brew — the resulting liquid resembled a British Porter ale so closely that the name stuck.

The stems are covered in recurved spines that catch on everything — clothing, skin, passing animals. This is deliberate: the plant uses passing animals and people to carry its seeds in the spiny pods that also catch on fur and fabric.
The most widely consumed fresh fruit in the world by volume — more mangoes are eaten globally each year than apples, bananas, or grapes.
The flowers look exactly like their name: soft, rounded, pink powder-puff heads made up of dozens of long, delicate stamens. There are no petals to speak of — the stamens are the show.
The intense purple color of every part of this plant — stems, leaves, and flowers — is caused by anthocyanin pigments that protect the plant from UV radiation in its exposed native habitat.

The species name glabra means smooth or hairless in Latin — distinguishing it from the more common Bougainvillea spectabilis, which has hairy bracts and stems. In practice the two are so commonly hybridized and mislabeled in the nursery trade that species-level ID in the garden is often unreliable.

The flowers produce one of the most vivid natural blue dyes known — used for centuries in Southeast Asian cooking to color rice, desserts, and beverages a striking blue or purple. Butterfly pea tea is vivid blue and turns pink when lemon juice is added — the color change is a pH reaction visible in a glass.

The common name describes the flower arrangement precisely: small white flowers nestled inside boat-shaped purple bracts, like a figure in a cradle. The bracts are the persistent ornamental feature; the actual flowers are brief.

One of the richest natural sources of vitamin C known — a single Acerola cherry contains approximately 65 times more vitamin C than an orange of similar weight. This is not approximate; it is documented nutritional analysis.

Flowers in the evening, when most ornamentals have stopped for the day. The white star-shaped flowers open as the light fades, releasing a sweet fragrance and revealing long purple stamens that give the plant its name.

A Florida native coastal shrub with one of the more distinctive seed pod structures in the collection — the pods constrict tightly between each seed, creating a beaded appearance that inspired both the common name and the species classification.

Named for its flowering trigger — rain. A summer rainstorm after a dry period causes mass blooming within days across an entire planting. The mechanism is thought to be related to soil temperature change and dissolved oxygen, though the precise trigger is still debated.
The seeds rattle in the dry pods — hence the name. The dry pods persist and provide a distinctive sound in wind.

One of the world's most invasive plants — listed among the 100 worst invasive species globally by the IUCN. Also one of the most productive butterfly nectar plants in Florida. Both of these things are true simultaneously, and that tension is the story.

A close relative of the Kapok — same subfamily, same spiny trunk, same strategy of flowering on bare branches before the leaves return. The Kapok stands to its left at the park; the comparison between the two is worth making.

An aquatic or semi-aquatic native creeping plant that forms dense mats at pond edges and in moist soil. The round penny-shaped leaves are arranged in whorls around the stem — the source of both common names.

A Florida native in the coffee family (Rubiaceae) — same family as coffee, Pentas, Firebush, and Ixora in the park collection — but not a source of true coffee. The small red fruits do contain caffeine in trace amounts, which is a family trait.

A small non-native groundcover, naturalized throughout Florida in disturbed areas, lawns, and roadsides. The tiny white star-shaped flowers cluster in tight whorls where the leaves meet the stem and feed small native bees and other insects.

Opens at four in the afternoon. Closes the next morning. The name is precise — it is one of the more reliable plant clocks in horticulture, triggered by declining light intensity rather than temperature.
One of the most recognizable groundcovers in Florida — the yellow daisy flowers and creeping mat habit are everywhere in landscaping, medians, and disturbed areas.

A low-growing groundcover with cheerful white to pale-pink, star-shaped flowers, often dismissed as a lawn weed. It belongs to the coffee family (Rubiaceae) and is widely called Florida snow for the way its blooms can cover a lawn, and Mexican clover.
A tiny orchid that grows as a lawn weed throughout warm-climate Florida — visible as a small rosette of flat leaves followed by a thin spike of inconspicuous white flowers.

The seeds rattle in the dry pods — hence the name. As the bean-like pods mature they inflate like small balloons and the loose seeds inside turn the plant into a natural maraca. A delightful sound for something so toxic.
The burgundy-red leaves are the display here — a deep, wine-red color unusual in the tropical foliage palette and striking against green neighbors.

Not related to orchids. The common name comes from the flower shape, which bears a passing resemblance. The actual family is Fabaceae — the legume family — making it a relative of beans, peas, and the park's own Royal Poinciana.

A native creeping groundcover that grows in lawns, path edges, and disturbed moist ground throughout Florida. The tiny white flower clusters are insignificant to the human eye but are a documented nectar source for small native bees and the larval host for several butterfly species including the White Peacock and Phaon Crescent.
The same two-lobed leaf as its close relative the Orchid Tree (Bauhinia variegata) nearby — use the leaf to find it, then the flower to tell them apart.
A small Asian composite weed — one of the smallest-flowered plants in the park, naturalized in disturbed soil and path edges. The tiny yellow flowers on thin branching stems are easy to overlook.

A Florida native coastal shrub that most visitors have seen along roadsides and waterways without knowing its name. The late-fall fluffy white seed heads — covering female plants in cotton-like masses — are how most people first notice it.

One of the most productive fall nectar plants for Florida pollinators. The yellow flower plumes in October and November arrive precisely when most other flowering plants are finishing for the year, providing a critical late-season nectar source.
The deeply dissected leaves and coral-red flowers create an unusual texture in the tropical garden — fine-cut foliage on a shrub that otherwise has a bold tropical presence.

The fragrant white flower clusters in spring give the common name — the scent is genuinely sweet and carries some distance. The berries that follow are eaten by birds and are responsible for why Sweet Viburnum is now classified as a FISC Category I invasive in Florida.
The color range of the Garden Croton is extraordinary by ornamental plant standards — a single leaf can include red, orange, yellow, green, and purple simultaneously, and no two plants look alike.
Low in the middle of Bird of Paradise Island, beneath the towering birds, sits the most historically precious plant in the park: an Amazon Lily handed down through the Baden family since the 1880s.

A Florida native shrub or small tree that earns its keep year-round. In late winter and early spring it covers itself in small white flower clusters, locally nicknamed Florida snow, that draw bees and butterflies.
